algorithms.name: Selection Sort
algorithms.analogy: Find the smallest card in the unsorted pile, move it to the sorted pile. Repeat until done.
algorithms.story: Imagine you're sorting a hand of playing cards. You scan all cards, find the lowest, place it at position 0. Then scan the remaining cards, find the next lowest, place it at position 1. You're always selecting the minimum from the unsorted portion and placing it at the boundary. Unlike Bubble Sort, Selection Sort makes exactly n-1 swaps total — useful when writes are expensive.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Number of swaps must be minimised (memory writes are costly — e.g. flash memory)
• Array is small and simplicity matters
• Teaching sorting: the 'scan and pick minimum' logic is intuitive
• When checking whether data is already sorted isn't useful
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Outer loop: i from 0 to n-2 (each position to fill)
2. Assume arr[i] is the minimum — save its index as minIdx
3. Inner loop: j from i+1 to n-1 — if arr[j] < arr[minIdx], update minIdx
4. After inner loop, swap arr[i] with arr[minIdx]
5. After pass i, elements [0..i] are sorted in their final positions
6. Repeat until i reaches n-2
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Selection sort always does O(n²) comparisons regardless of input — no early exit possible
• Key advantage over Bubble Sort: exactly n-1 swaps total (minimum possible for a comparison sort)
• It is NOT stable: swapping can change relative order of equal elements
• In practice, never use for large n — use merge sort or Arrays.sort()
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Minimising Write Operations","desc":"In systems where writes are expensive (e.g., EEPROM, flash storage), selection sort's O(n) swaps beats bubble sort's O(n²) swaps."},{"title":"Small Embedded Systems","desc":"When sorting 5-10 elements in microcontroller firmware, selection sort's simplicity and predictable O(n²) is fine and easy to verify."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Thinking selection sort is stable — it isn't: swapping with the minimum can move equal elements out of order","Forgetting to check minIdx != i before swapping — unnecessary swaps don't break correctness but waste time","Using selection sort on large arrays in production code"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Bidirectional Selection Sort","desc":"Each pass finds both the minimum and maximum and places both simultaneously — halves the number of passes"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Makes exactly n-1 swaps total — the theoretical minimum for a comparison sort that must move elements into place. The ONLY interview-worthy reason to discuss Selection Sort is minimum write operations (flash memory). Everything else is inferior to merge sort.
algorithms.patternSignal: minimum writes, minimize swaps, flash memory sort, scan and pick minimum, O(1) space simple sort
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Sort an Array (Selection Sort)","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Implement the scan-and-pick-minimum loop — good warm-up"},{"name":"Minimum Number of Swaps to Sort Array","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Count cycles in the permutation — each cycle of length L needs L-1 swaps"}]

algorithms.name: Insertion Sort
algorithms.analogy: Sort a hand of cards as you pick them up — slide each new card left until it's in the right spot.
algorithms.story: You're at a card table, picking up one card at a time. You hold a sorted hand and insert each new card into its correct position by shifting cards right. This is insertion sort. The key insight: as you insert the k-th card, the first k-1 cards are already sorted. Insertion sort is optimal for nearly-sorted arrays — Java's TimSort uses it for small subarrays precisely because of this property.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Array is nearly sorted — O(n) when few elements are out of place
• Online sorting — elements arrive one at a time and need to be sorted as they come
• Small arrays (n < 20) — used as base case in TimSort and IntroSort
• Stable sort with O(1) space needed
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Treat arr[0] as a sorted subarray of size 1
2. For i from 1 to n-1:
   a. Save arr[i] as 'key'
   b. Set j = i-1
   c. While j >= 0 AND arr[j] > key: shift arr[j] right to arr[j+1], decrement j
   d. Place key at arr[j+1]
3. After each pass, arr[0..i] is sorted
4. Repeat until i = n-1
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Insertion sort is the fastest for n <= 10-15 — this is why TimSort uses it as the base case
• It is adaptive: O(n + d) where d = number of inversions. Nearly sorted → nearly O(n)
• It is online: can sort a list as it receives elements, unlike merge sort which needs all elements
• Binary insertion sort reduces comparisons but NOT the number of shifts — still O(n²) total work
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"TimSort Base Case","desc":"Java's Arrays.sort() and Python's sorted() both use TimSort which switches to insertion sort for runs smaller than ~32 elements."},{"title":"Online Sorting","desc":"If you're receiving stock ticker data and need to maintain a sorted list, insertion sort inserts each new element in O(n) — no need to re-sort from scratch."},{"title":"Nearly-Sorted Restoration","desc":"After a small update to a sorted dataset (e.g., one new record inserted), insertion sort fixes the order in O(n) rather than O(n log n)."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Shifting vs swapping: insertion sort shifts elements (arr[j+1] = arr[j]), not swapping — shifting is O(1) extra space, swapping would also work but shifts are cleaner","While loop condition order: j >= 0 must come BEFORE arr[j] > key to avoid ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException","Confusing with selection sort: insertion sort shifts into a sorted prefix; selection sort picks the minimum each pass"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Shell Sort","desc":"Generalisation of insertion sort using gap sequences — sorts elements far apart first, then reduces gap. O(n^1.5) average — much faster than O(n²)"},{"name":"Binary Insertion Sort","desc":"Uses binary search to find the insertion position — reduces comparisons to O(n log n) but total work is still O(n²) due to shifting"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Optimal for nearly-sorted data: O(n + d) where d = number of inversions. That's why TimSort uses it for small subarrays (n < 32). Also ONLINE — can sort a stream without buffering all elements first. No other O(n log n) sort has these two properties.
algorithms.patternSignal: nearly sorted, online sorting, small array base case, stable sort O(1) space, stream of elements, TimSort base case
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Sort an Array (Insertion Sort)","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Implement the shift-left inner loop — shift larger elements right, insert at correct position"},{"name":"Sort Nearly Sorted Array (K-sorted)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Each element is at most K positions away — use a min-heap of size K+1 for O(n log K)"}]

algorithms.name: Heap Sort
algorithms.analogy: Build a leaderboard (max-heap), pull off the champion repeatedly, and they line up in sorted order.
algorithms.story: Imagine a tournament bracket. You build a max-heap — a structure where the largest element is always at the root. You extract the root (largest), put it at the end of the array, shrink the heap, and restore the heap property. Repeat. After n extractions, the array is sorted in ascending order. Heap Sort gives you O(n log n) guaranteed with O(1) space — the rare combination that makes it theoretically ideal.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Guaranteed O(n log n) with O(1) space (no recursion stack like merge sort)
• Memory is constrained and merge sort's O(n) extra space is too expensive
• You already need a heap for another purpose (e.g. priority queue)
• Worst-case performance guarantee matters AND in-place is required
algorithms.howItWorks:
Phase 1 — Build max-heap (heapify):
1. Start from last non-leaf node (n/2 - 1), heapify down to root
2. After this, arr[0] is the maximum element

Phase 2 — Extract-sort:
3. For i from n-1 down to 1:
   a. Swap arr[0] (max) with arr[i] (last unsorted element)
   b. Reduce heap size by 1
   c. Heapify arr[0] downward to restore heap property
4. After each extraction, arr[i] is in its final sorted position
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Heap sort is NOT used in practice because of poor cache performance — the heap accesses memory non-sequentially
• But the O(n log n) worst-case + O(1) space makes it theoretically the best sorting algorithm
• Building the heap is O(n) — not O(n log n) — this is a subtle point interviewers love
• Heap sort is unstable: the heapify step can change the relative order of equal elements
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Priority Queue Implementation","desc":"Java's PriorityQueue is a min-heap. Understanding heap sort means understanding PriorityQueue internals, which are tested in interviews."},{"title":"Top-K Problems","desc":"Finding the K largest elements uses a min-heap of size K — related to heap sort's extract operation."},{"title":"Memory-Constrained Sorting","desc":"In systems where allocating an O(n) auxiliary array is too expensive (embedded, kernel code), heap sort is the only O(n log n) in-place option."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Wrong child indices: left = 2*i+1, right = 2*i+2 for 0-indexed arrays (not 2*i, 2*i+1)","Starting heapify from wrong index: must start from n/2 - 1 (last non-leaf), not n-1","Forgetting to call heapify after swap in Phase 2 — the heap property is broken after each extraction","Using heap sort when stability matters — it is NOT a stable sort"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Min-Heap Sort","desc":"Use a min-heap to sort in descending order instead of ascending"},{"name":"Smoothsort","desc":"Adaptive variant of heap sort that achieves O(n) on nearly-sorted data while maintaining O(n log n) worst case"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Building the heap is O(n), NOT O(n log n) — common interview misconception. Heap sort is the only O(n log n) IN-PLACE sort with O(1) space, but poor cache performance makes it slower than quicksort in practice. Child indices are 2*i+1 and 2*i+2 (0-indexed) — getting this wrong breaks heapify.
algorithms.patternSignal: guaranteed O(n log n) in-place, O(1) space sort, no recursion stack, max-heap from array, heapify, sort without extra memory
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Sort an Array (Heap Sort)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Phase 1: build max-heap from n/2-1 downward. Phase 2: extract max to end n-1 times"},{"name":"Last Stone Weight","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Max-heap: repeatedly take the two heaviest stones"},{"name":"Kth Largest Element in an Array","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Partial heap sort — only extract max K times instead of n times"}]

algorithms.name: Counting Sort
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(n + k) · k = range of values
algorithms.analogy: Count how many of each number exist, then reconstruct — no comparisons needed.
algorithms.story: You're a teacher grading 100 tests where scores are integers from 0 to 100. Instead of sorting the test papers, you count: '3 students got 70, 5 got 85...' and write names in order from that count. You never compared two papers directly. Counting Sort uses this insight to break the O(n log n) barrier for comparison sorts — it runs in O(n + k) where k is the range of values.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Values are integers (or can be mapped to integers) in a known, small range [0..k]
• k is not much larger than n (O(n + k) must be better than O(n log n))
• Stable sort needed (equal elements keep original order)
• Sorting characters, small non-negative integers, or grades
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Find max value k in the array
2. Create count array of size k+1, initialised to 0
3. Count occurrences: for each element arr[i], increment count[arr[i]]
4. Prefix sum: for i from 1 to k, count[i] += count[i-1] (now count[i] = number of elements <= i)
5. Build output: traverse arr right-to-left, place arr[i] at output[count[arr[i]]-1], decrement count[arr[i]]
6. Copy output back to arr
algorithms.interviewTips:
• The key constraint: values must be non-negative integers in a bounded range
• Explain WHY it breaks the O(n log n) barrier: it doesn't use comparisons — uses counting/indexing
• k should be O(n) for counting sort to be worth it. If k = 10^9, it's worse than merge sort
• Used internally in Radix Sort — explain the connection
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Sorting Characters","desc":"Sorting a string's characters (k=26 for lowercase) is O(n) with counting sort — used in anagram checks."},{"title":"Histogram / Frequency Analysis","desc":"The count array itself is a frequency histogram — you get both sorted output and frequency data in one pass."},{"title":"Radix Sort Base","desc":"Radix sort applies counting sort digit-by-digit on each decimal/binary position, requiring a stable counting sort implementation."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Assuming values start at 0: if values are in range [-100..100], shift all values by min to map to [0..200]","Memory explosion: if k = 10^9, the count array requires gigabytes — counting sort is only practical for small k","Simple version (reconstruct from counts) is NOT stable: if stability matters, use the prefix-sum + right-to-left technique"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Relative Counting Sort","desc":"Handle arbitrary integer ranges by shifting values to start at 0: offset = min(arr), value → value - offset"},{"name":"Radix Sort","desc":"Applies counting sort digit-by-digit from least significant to most significant — sorts n-digit integers in O(d*(n+k)) time"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Breaks the O(n log n) barrier by not using comparisons. The stable version requires prefix-sum trick + right-to-left traversal. Without stability, Radix Sort using counting sort as a subroutine fails. O(n+k) advantage only holds when k = O(n) — if k = 10^9, it's worse than merge sort.
algorithms.patternSignal: integer keys bounded range, O(n+k) sort, stable sort without comparisons, sort characters, frequency histogram, radix sort base
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Counting sort: count 0s, 1s, 2s then overwrite — or use three-way partition in O(1) space"},{"name":"Rank Transform of an Array","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Sort, assign ranks 1..n, then map back to original positions"},{"name":"Relative Sort Array","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Counting sort for elements in order1, append remaining in sorted order"}]

algorithms.name: Radix Sort
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(n + k) · d = digits, k = base
algorithms.analogy: Sort a pile of punch cards first by last column, then second-to-last, then first — the whole pile ends up sorted.
algorithms.story: Old tabulating machines sorted punch cards by running the deck through d times — once per column, from rightmost to leftmost. After d passes, the entire deck was sorted. Radix Sort applies this: sort all numbers by their units digit, then tens, then hundreds. Because each digit-sort is stable (equal digits preserve prior order), the final result is fully sorted. For 32-bit integers with base 256, d=4 — just 4 passes over the array.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Sorting large arrays of integers where d*k is small compared to n*log(n)
• Numbers have a known, fixed number of digits (e.g., phone numbers, IP addresses, IDs)
• Stable sort of fixed-length strings or keys
• When O(n log n) comparison sorts aren't fast enough for your use case
algorithms.howItWorks:
LSD (Least Significant Digit) Radix Sort:
1. For each digit position d from least significant to most significant:
   a. Extract digit at position d from each element: (arr[i] / 10^d) % 10
   b. Apply stable counting sort on those digit values
   c. The array is now sorted by all digits from d to the most recently processed
2. After processing all digit positions, array is fully sorted
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Radix sort is NOT comparison-based — explain why it can beat O(n log n)
• For 32-bit integers with base-256, d=4 passes — very fast in practice
• The digit sort MUST be stable — without stability, LSD radix sort produces wrong results
• MSD (Most Significant Digit) radix sort is recursive and works better for strings
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Sorting Phone Numbers / IDs","desc":"All IDs have the same number of digits — radix sort sorts 10 million 10-digit IDs in 10 passes of O(n) each."},{"title":"Suffix Array Construction","desc":"DC3/Skew algorithm for suffix arrays uses radix sort internally for O(n) total construction time."},{"title":"Network Packet Classification","desc":"Sorting IP addresses (fixed 32-bit width) by multiple fields uses a radix-sort-like approach."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using unstable counting sort for each digit pass — this produces wrong results; the digit sort MUST be stable","Not handling negative numbers: standard radix sort only works for non-negative integers; negatives need special handling","Wrong exp termination: the loop condition max/exp > 0 ensures all digit positions are covered"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"MSD Radix Sort","desc":"Most Significant Digit first — recursive, good for variable-length strings, allows early termination"},{"name":"Base-256 Radix Sort","desc":"Process a byte (8 bits) at a time — only 4 passes for 32-bit integers with k=256 buckets"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: O(d*(n+k)) where d=digits, k=base. For 32-bit integers with base 256: d=4, k=256 — just 4 passes over the entire array. The counting sort for each pass MUST be stable — without stability LSD radix sort produces wrong results because digit-order built up from earlier passes gets destroyed.
algorithms.patternSignal: fixed-length integers, digit-by-digit sort, sort phone numbers or IDs, linear sort, stable non-comparison sort, sort by multiple keys
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Maximum Gap","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Radix sort the array, then scan consecutive elements — O(n) solution"},{"name":"Sort Array by Number of Set Bits","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Counting sort by popcount — O(n+32) since max 32 bits"}]

algorithms.name: Bucket Sort
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(n + k) · k = number of buckets
algorithms.analogy: Sort exam scores by grouping them into grade bands, sort each band separately, then concatenate.
algorithms.story: You're sorting 1000 floating-point numbers uniformly distributed between 0 and 1. Instead of comparing all pairs, you divide the range [0,1] into 10 buckets: [0,0.1), [0.1,0.2), ... Each bucket gets roughly 100 elements. Sort each bucket (with insertion sort — nearly sorted). Concatenate. Because elements are uniformly distributed, each bucket has O(n/k) elements and sorting each takes O(n/k)². With k=n buckets, total time is O(n). This is the intuition behind Bucket Sort.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Input is uniformly distributed over a known range (floats in [0,1], integers in [min,max])
• Range can be divided into k roughly equal-sized buckets
• Sorting floating-point numbers efficiently
• When distribution is known and hash function can evenly distribute elements
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create k empty buckets (k = n typically)
2. For each element, compute its bucket: bucket = (int)(element * k / (max + 1))
3. Insert each element into its corresponding bucket
4. Sort each bucket (insertion sort is fine — buckets are small)
5. Concatenate all buckets in order to get the sorted array
algorithms.interviewTips:
• O(n) average depends on uniform distribution — ask the interviewer about input distribution
• Worst case O(n²) happens when all elements fall into one bucket
• Unlike counting sort, bucket sort works on floating-point numbers
• The bucket sort → hash sort connection: bucket assignment is essentially a hash function
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Sorting Floating-Point Scores","desc":"Sorting 1M exam scores in [0.0, 100.0] with known uniform distribution — bucket sort is O(n) vs O(n log n) for comparison sorts."},{"title":"Geographic Clustering","desc":"Sorting GPS coordinates (latitude/longitude) into grid cells before spatial queries uses bucket-sort-like range partitioning."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Assuming O(n) without verifying uniform distribution — skewed input causes all elements in one bucket, giving O(n²)","Edge case: element equal to max value maps to bucket n, which is out of bounds — handle explicitly","Using a complex O(n log n) sort for each bucket defeats the purpose — use insertion sort for small buckets"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Flash Sort","desc":"Optimised bucket sort that estimates bucket sizes from a sample of the data before distributing all elements"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: O(n) ONLY when input is uniformly distributed. Worst case O(n²) if all elements fall into one bucket. Unlike counting sort, handles floating-point numbers. Always clarify input distribution before claiming O(n) — interviewers will probe this.
algorithms.patternSignal: uniformly distributed floats, range partitioning, O(n) sort with known distribution, sort floats in [0,1], geographic clustering
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Maximum Gap","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Pigeonhole: with n numbers in range [min,max], gap must be at least (max-min)/(n-1) — use buckets of that size"},{"name":"Top K Frequent Elements","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Bucket sort by frequency: frequency f → bucket[f] (max frequency <= n)"}]
